statement.markdown: Given a matrix of size M x N consisting of all 0s and 1s, return the number of square submatrices with all 1s.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains two integers M and N, representing the number of rows and columns in the matrix respectively.
Each of the next M lines contains N space-separated integers representing the matrix rows.
statement.outputFormat: A single integer representing the count of square submatrices with all 1s.

examples.explanation: There are 10 squares of size 1x1, 4 squares of size 2x2, and 1 square of size 3x3. Total = 10 + 4 + 1 = 15.
There are 6 squares of size 1x1 and 1 square of size 2x2. Total = 6 + 1 = 7.

hints.text: Consider iterating over each cell in the matrix, and for each cell, calculate the number of square submatrices of all sizes that can be formed with that cell as the top-left corner.

To calculate the number of square submatrices for a given cell, you can use a prefix sum or cumulative sum approach. This will allow you to efficiently calculate the sum of all elements in a submatrix.

To efficiently calculate the sum of all elements in a submatrix, you can use a technique where you maintain a running sum of the elements in each row and column. This will allow you to quickly calculate the sum of any submatrix.

Consider using a dynamic programming approach to solve this problem. You can use a 2D array to store the cumulative sum of elements in each submatrix, and then use this array to calculate the total number of square submatrices.

The key to solving this problem is to realize that the number of square submatrices of a given size that can be formed with a cell as the top-left corner is equal to the product of the number of square submatrices of that size that can be formed with the cell to the left and the cell above it.

editorial.title: Counting Square Submatrices with All 1s: From Brute Force to Dynamic Programming
editorial.intuition: When you look at a binary matrix, the only thing that matters for a square ending at a cell is whether its three neighbours (top, left, and top‑left) can also support a square. This local property lets us build larger squares incrementally.
editorial.bruteForce: A naive approach would enumerate every possible top‑left corner, try all side lengths, and check all cells inside each candidate square. That is O(m*n*min(m,n)^2) time and quickly becomes infeasible even for moderate sizes. It is correct because it literally tests every square, but its quadratic factor in the side length makes it too slow for 300x300 matrices.
editorial.betterSolution: The key observation is that the size of the largest square ending at (i,j) depends only on the sizes at (i-1,j), (i,j-1), and (i-1,j-1). By computing these values in a single pass and reusing the input matrix to store the DP values, we achieve linear time and constant extra space.
editorial.optimalSolution: For each cell containing a 1, set its DP value to 1 plus the minimum of its three neighbours’ DP values; otherwise set it to 0. Accumulate these DP values to get the total number of all‑1 squares. This runs in O(m*n) time and O(1) additional space.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Let dp[i][j] be the size of the largest all‑1 square whose bottom‑right corner is (i,j). If mat[i][j]==0, no such square exists, so dp[i][j]=0. If mat[i][j]==1, any square ending at (i,j) must also end at the three neighbours. The largest possible side length is therefore 1 plus the smallest of the three neighbour sizes, because that neighbour limits how far the square can extend. By induction over rows and columns, the recurrence correctly computes dp[i][j] for every cell, and summing all dp[i][j] counts every square exactly once.
editorial.dryRun: Consider a 3x3 matrix of all 1s. The DP table evolves as: row0: [1,1,1]; row1: [1,2,2]; row2: [1,2,3]. Summing gives 1+1+1+1+2+2+1+2+3=14, which matches the 14 all‑1 squares in the matrix.
commonMistakes: Assuming the DP value can be computed without considering all three neighbours; missing the min operation leads to overcounting.
Using a separate DP array and forgetting to reset cells to 0 when the input is 0, causing stale values to propagate.
Implementing the recurrence with 1‑based indices but accessing 0‑based neighbours, leading to index errors.
Failing to accumulate the answer inside the loop, resulting in only the largest square being counted.
Interpreting the DP value as the number of squares instead of the side length, which would require an extra summation of 1+2+…+dp[i][j].
Using recursion without memoization, which re‑computes subproblems and defeats the linear‑time goal.
